title: Cookie Chain — Introduction
content:
Cookie Chain — Introduction

Cookie Chain is a community-run Solana fork. It started as a Solana fork. The original group of community devs built a thriving chain and ecosystem. When the core team's vision stopped aligning with the community, the community forked the fork and kept shipping.

Today, Cookie Chain is operated by an expanding multi-validator set with COOK as the native asset. Real validators. Real governance. Real shipping.

What makes Cookie Chain different:
- Solana compatible: SPL tokens, programs, and tooling work out of the box.
- Community operated: no foundation gate, no single operator.
- Multi-sig bridge: COOK moves between Solana and Cookie Chain through an m-of-n community multi-sig — no single key controls the vault.
- Sub-second finality: ~1s block times with Solana-style consensus.
- Minimal fees: fees paid in COOK, priced for builders and users, not extractors.
- Dirt-cheap program deploys: deploying a program on Cookie Chain costs next to nothing compared to Solana mainnet.

title: Cookie Chain — Architecture & Consensus
content:
Cookie Chain — Architecture & Consensus

Cookie Chain inherits SVM architecture with PoH, Tower BFT, Turbine, Gulf Stream, Sealevel, and pipelining.

Proof of History (PoH): A verifiable delay function acts as a cryptographic clock and orders events before consensus runs.

Tower BFT: A PoH-optimized PBFT variant lets validators vote on the PoH sequence, reducing messaging overhead and supporting sub-second finality.

Turbine: block propagation via randomized shredding.
Gulf Stream: mempool-less transaction forwarding directly to the upcoming leader.
Sealevel: parallel program execution across CPU cores.
Pipelining: staged transaction validation for steady throughput.

Block time & finality:
- Target block time: ~80-140 ms
- Finality is sub-second under normal conditions.
- Consensus is produced by an expanding set of independent validators.

title: Introducing the Cookie Recipe — Cookie Chain Origin Story
content:
Introducing the Cookie Recipe 🍪
Cookie Chain belongs to the third category.
Tech (Tekh) is the Meme, Lore, Cookies.
Cookie Chain was born from frustration, survival, and belief.
Not belief in marketing. Belief in builders.
Before Cookie Chain existed, there was Gorbagana… A chaotic experimental ecosystem forked from Solana. It attracted developers because it felt raw, fast, and open. People built strange things. Fun things. Real things.
But eventually the experiment stopped feeling decentralized. The community slowly realized something painful: The ecosystem depended too heavily on a centralized operational structure. One man’s control of the chain. Many empty promises. One hand gets all of the money that chain gets. And then the chain halted... Not once. Twice. For a combined total of nearly 96 hours. During these outages, the community received almost no communication. Builders were left in uncertainty while the ecosystem stood frozen.
At the same time, many community developers felt abandoned. The people building applications, infrastructure, and liquidity for the ecosystem were rarely supported, while chain revenues continuously flowed toward the core structure itself.
That moment changed everything.
Cookie Chain was born from the idea that ecosystems should belong to communities, not gatekeepers.
How Did Cookie Chain Idea Come to Life?
When Gorbagana halted, something important became clear: A blockchain is not its servers. A blockchain is not its branding. A blockchain is its people.
Fortunately, the community still possessed something powerful: The snapshot. The state survived. The builders survived. And that meant the ecosystem itself could survive.
At that moment, community developers decided they had seen enough. Enough silence. Enough dependency. Enough centralized control over an ecosystem built by its users.
So the builders chose to fork the chain themselves and create something different: A truly community-friendly chain where infrastructure, validators, developers, and treasury systems belong to the ecosystem. Not to rulers.
That chain became Cookie Chain.
From Snapshot to Survival
Cookie Chain is forked directly from Gorbagana, which itself was forked from Solana. A fork of a fork.
But this is more than copied code. It is preserved history. When Cookie Chain launches, users will discover something important: Their assets already exist there. All user balances and states from Gorbagana are cloned directly into Cookie Chain. Think of it almost like a massive community airdrop. A second chance. A new culture. A fresh beginning.
After the fork, Cookie Chain assets become fully independent from Gorbagana assets. Markets, liquidity, and prices evolve separately. The histories are shared. The future is not.
Joining Cookie Chain is simple: Users only need to switch their RPC endpoint to:
https://rpc.cookiescan.io
State Cloning & The Community Airdrop
Cookie Chain users are not starting from zero. The ecosystem history matters. Wallets matter. Communities matter. Builders matter.
That is why the state itself was preserved. The chain remembers who participated. Many users can think of Cookie Chain as receiving a parallel ecosystem version of their existing Gorbagana assets. Same history. Different future.
The Community Vaults
Cookie Chain is structured around community-controlled treasury systems rather than centralized ownership. The primary governance structure is controlled through a Squads Protocol Multisig:
BQVAdo6Ls2rYAggzXu38peyYg8BtQASUn6bnm9FfS5Ab
Within this multisig exists:

Vault 0 (Bridge Vault): G3mm95M4ns7mk8oseWGJnirvgyMahMz3vZEUhdJn8oGX — contains the non-circulating cCOOK supply intended for bridge infrastructure.
Vault 1 (Cookie Jar): 568tU9FMksJDxjkLBjWisSA4J4C5uPH87NCCkyREwrxe — Protocols can voluntarily contribute a portion of their on-chain revenue into this shared ecosystem vault to fund developers, validators, infrastructure, and builders.

Even the future Pump.fun creator fees generated by sCOOK trading on Solana will flow into protected multisig-controlled treasury systems dedicated entirely to community growth.
Why Cookie Chain Has No Rulers
The remaining 73% of the supply is not privately owned. It is community-controlled.
Developers Will Cook
Cookie Chain is designed for builders aka cookers:

~400ms block times
Negligible deployment costs
Minimal friction
Open bridge infrastructure available from day one

The Builders Are Moving 🍪
Cookie Chain is not being built by a single team. It is being rebuilt by an entire ecosystem.
Many original Gorbagana community developers are migrating with their projects:

Trashbin AMM → CookieSwap AMM
Dumpsack Wallet → Morsel Wallet
Trashscan → CookieScan
Bang.meme (launchpad)
TrashDAQ → Candy Shop (DEX aggregator)
CookBook (.cook domain service)
CookLock (native token vesting and locking protocol)

The ecosystem roadmap includes Public DAS Indexer infrastructure, Native Airdrop Applications, Advanced Charting systems, Perpetual Exchanges, Prediction Markets, Community-native bridge infrastructure, and Nightly Wallet integration.
The Multi-Validator Future
Cookie Chain believes chains should survive beyond individuals. Validator diversity and infrastructure resilience matter. No single baker should control the oven.
Final Words
Cookie Chain is not trying to become another polished corporate blockchain. It is something stranger. A preserved ecosystem. A forked culture. A community experiment that refused to disappear.
The chain halted. But the builders kept cooking.
Community will cook. 🍪

title: Gorbagana Cash — Documentation Overview
content:
Docs
Read the manual. Eat the cookie.
Everything needed to understand the fork, join the network, and build on GCASH.
Docs
Introduction
The story of Gorbagana Cash, why the fork exists, and what the community is continuing.
Gorbagana Cash is a community-owned hard fork of Gorbagana. It keeps the historical continuity of the chain, but moves the future away from the old single-operator control model.
The community went through drama, instability, and uncertainty. When the original Gorbagana chain was halted by Rick, the network could have ended there. Fortunately, a backup snapshot survived.
That snapshot became the foundation for a community-led hard fork. The result is simple: same chain history, new community future. GCASH is the continuation of the state, the users, and the builders who stayed.
Core idea
Same chain history. New community future.

title: Gorbagana Cash — For Degens
content:
Use The Chain
For degens
If you know Solana, you already understand the base experience. The apps and culture are what change.
Docs
For degens
If you know Solana, you already understand the base experience. The apps and culture are what change.
Gorbagana Cash behaves like Solana at the network level. Wallet flows, transactions, and dApp interactions should feel familiar. What changes is the ecosystem, the apps, and the community culture around the chain.
Bridge assets into Gorbagana Cash, then visit the ecosystem page to find the dApps you want to use. Swap, explore, mint, build, or just watch the cookie jar fill up.
Simple path
Bridge in. Pick a dApp. Use GCASH like a Solana-style network with a different ecosystem.
